sentences.text: Well, I didn't have a personal meeting with him, as such.
He came to visit the houses, and I was part of a group that greeted him.
I-- I found him enormously inspirational.
I really felt he was larger than life.
I think I sensed what he was doing more than I understood what he was doing.

I mean, he really left behind the comfort and luxury and safety of Sweden and came to the hell that Budapest was in the summer of 1944, because he felt a common bond with human beings who were of a different ethnicity, different religion, different background, different situation from him.
But he felt a personal obligation to help.
And quite frankly, he has been the dominant theme of my life.
He has been the dominant influence on my life more than anybody else, more than my parents, more than any friends, more than any teachers.
If-- if-- if I am asked, at the end of my life, who is the person who shaped you and your values and your actions and your views and your energies, it's Wallenberg.
I-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] photo and his large facsimile of the stamp we-- we'll soon see in every post office.
I have a large sign saying 'Wallenberg Way' that Washington gave me as a-- a-- a remembrance of the fact that the Holocaust Museum is on Wallenberg Place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, Wallenberg tied the lives of my wife and my own life together, because she has devoted years of her life to Wallenberg.
She succeeded in making him a household word, and she failed in freeing him-- liberating him, because we are both convinced he was alive when my wife started the work.
And I am immensely grateful that I could introduce legislation, which was my first piece of legislation, making him an honorary citizen.
We now have a statue in the capital that my wife arranged, and I passed the legislation.
We have the postage stamp coming up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He will be, for generations, an inspiration of what the code of conduct of human beings needs to be, that we are our brothers' and sisters' keepers.
Well, I wasn't a child.
I was 16.
I was a very mature, very old 16.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were very conscious of the fact that we were living in this house, because through Wallenberg, this house was under-- theoretically-- the protection of the Royal Swedish government.
We were aware of the fact that there were scores of such houses, thousands and thousands of people, who were not deported to Auschwitz because of this man and what this man did.

And for a young boy-- 16-year-old boy-- who had given up all hope of surviving the war, as the year 1944 moved on, the opportunity of helping in a-- in a tiny, modest way, this incredible work that he was doing was-- it-- it gave meaning to what I thought was the last year of my life.
I remember him as a very serious person.
I remember him as a person with a tremendous amount of personal presence with an authority.
I remember him as someone I would have followed in any direction, because I-- I sensed that he was motivated by the finest of instincts.
He was in his 30s.
I-- but I don't want to-- to give the impression for a moment that I was a collaborator of his or a friend of his.
I mean, I was a-- a totally unimportant young person who looked up to him and who admired him, and who was thrilled to be able to-- to-- to assist in the most menial ways.
Yes.
No.
No, I didn't.
I saw him at the railroad station on one occasion.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and it's very difficult for me now to separate the pictures I-- I have seen since and what I remember from 1944.
I mean, it's more than a half a century ago.
And I can only say that he dominated my thinking and my actions during those months of-- the latter half of 1944, the first few weeks of '45.
The Russians liberated Pest first, Buda about a months later in mid-January.
And he was arrested-- which I didn't know at the time-- on January 17.
So I really was conscious of his-- his work for about a half a year.
No.
I was not at the level where, you know, I would-- I would know any of these things.
Yes, I believe I knew who Eichmann was.
I-- I-- I knew-- I believe I knew the name Eichmann, but I wouldn't swear to it.

It's so difficult, you know, after all these decades and after all the reading I have done, to sort of try to project back to the year 1944 and-- and remember what my frame of mind was or what my scope of knowledge was.
It was very limited.
It was very limited.
I was-- like everybody else around me, I was living from day to day.
There were no long-term plans.
I-- I didn't when-- when dawn broke that I will end that day still alive.
No, I did not know.
Protected houses.
Swedish protected houses.
No.
No, they were gone.
I don't know.
They were gone.
The only person I know-- the two people I knew-- and I lived the last few months of the war period with them-- were my mother's number-- my mother's family had four children.
My-- her parents had four children.
The oldest child was a tremendously impressive woman.
Her name was Wilma, and our younger daughter reminds me of her very much-- beautiful, statuesque, decisive, strong.
She perished.
The number two daughter, Margaret, survived the war in this apartment with me.
Her husband, Alexander, survived the war in this apartment with me.
The third sister was my mother, who perished.
I don't know how and when.
And the fourth one was her younger brother, who perished on the Russian front.
The only two people from the family that I had contact with-- and it was more than contact with, because after my aunt's husband died, I arranged for her to go to Vienna.
And I flew to Vienna and brought her back with me.
I held her hand in the plane all the way from Vienna to New York.
And she had blind faith in me.
She was a very-- she was a very-- she was a musician and the teacher of music and a lovely woman.
And I love-- I loved all of them very much.
There's very, very-- I-- I mean, I really had three mothers, both my mother and her two sisters.
And-- and there was really no difference.
We were so close.
She came with me to San Francisco, and she lived here with us in San Francisco.

And in retrospect, I'm not sure I did the right thing, because while we gave her as loving an environment as was humanly possible, she was at an age where, perhaps, she should have stayed with her friends and in a place with familiar language and customs and food and what not.
I was in Los Angeles on university business when I heard that-- that she had a heart attack and passed away here in-- in San Francisco.
Those three boys who died in labor camp were--
Yes.
Two of them were her children, and one of them was the child of the other sister.
There's no date, really.
There is no date.
There was a gradual awareness that-- that-- you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no-- no sign of any kind.
And-- and-- I-- I must admit that I-- I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was not surprised.
I was not surprised.
I-- I-- I threw myself into reading about this period as soon as the war was over, and articles and books were beginning to appear.
And-- and that process hasn't stopped.
And I don't think I understand it any better now than I did in 1945 of what happened, because there is no rational way of explaining it.
I'm a very rational person.
And while the enormity of the literature add to our understanding of the details and the nuances, if you had-- if you read 50 Holocaust memoirs, you could have read 500 Holocaust memoirs.
And as this project, it-- it-- it fills in bits of the mosaic, but it really doesn't explain anything.
No.
No.
No.
One of my grandparents may have been alive when I was born, but the answer is no.
I never knew them.
Well, the war ended for me in January of 1945.
I-- [LAUGHS] as-- as I have done since, I had many simultaneous jobs.
One of my jobs was to have a-- not a push cart, but a pull cart.
I was the horse that pulled this little cart.
And I got a job from-- from a Jewish relief agency picking up sacks of flour at the railroad station and taking them to the bakery.
So I played the-- the role of the horse in this undertaking for a long, long period of time.

Ironically, while I was working as a horse, I simultaneously became a professor of English to the Soviet general who was the commanding general of the troops that liberated Budapest, because he wanted to learn English.
And somehow, he found out that I was quite fluent in English.
So I had a-- a rather interesting experience.
I did my horse job in the morning, and I-- I-- I looked like a snowman because the flour was all over my horrible clothing.
I mean, I can't say 'clothes' because it-- I had rags.
And then I had a very good friend who was smaller than I, but he had a decent suit.
And I sort of barely fit into this suit.
And I-- I put on a shirt and a necktie and this horribly ill-fitting suit, and the Soviet commanding general's automobile came for me.
And I sat in the automobile as the professor, a very young professor.
I was 17 by then.
And I was taken to Soviet military headquarters, and I gave an English lesson to the general.
I also had a third job which I enjoyed very much.
It almost had a follow up.
In this apartment, which used to belong to my aunt and uncle but was a community property, there was a very attractive young couple.
Their name was Budai.
And the man had been a medical student.
And he decided that he will set up a medical shop.
I can't really say a doctor's office, because he was not a doctor.
And he had hidden some white medical jackets, and he had some stethoscopes.
And I remember, we did a-- we made a sign that we put on the door which said, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means 'physician.' And he became a sort of a physician to the Russian troops, and I was his assistant.
So I-- I had a variety of costumes during the day.
I had my professorial costume, my horse costume, and then I had my medical costume.
And I loved having the stethoscope hanging around my neck.
I didn't know what to do with it, but I was assisting him with-- with his medical attention.
So I hope he didn't hurt too many people.
We conceivably might have helped some.
I'm not sure of that.
What's he Jewish?
Yes.
Yes, he was.
His name was Budai-- Budai Laszlo.
Yes.
The artillery shelling became more and more intense over the course of the years.
The Soviet troops were moving closer and closer, and it was obvious that it was only a matter of days before we would be liberated.
And one very early morning, it was still pitch dark, 3 o'clock, 4 o'clock, we heard a great commotion.
And suddenly, Russian soldiers burst into the-- the apartment house.
And I don't know how.
And up to this day, I don't know how.
They knew that these were Jews.
I don't know if they knew about Wallenberg or not, but they knew that these were Jews who were hiding there, living there, waiting for the Soviet troops to come in.
And they made it very clear that they will not harm us.
And they, in fact, did not.
Subsequently, and they gave us-- I remember one of the things we got was a large sack of potatoes from them.
One of the things that-- that became a very interesting aspect of my life as 1945 winter gave way to spring, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] everybody was poor.
And the criminal elements could not take your jewelery or-- or valuables because you had no jewelry, and you had no valuables.
But they took your clothes.
And people sort of walked in the middle of the street.
There were no street cars or any other transportation.
And that the newspapers were full of these hilarious cartoons of the husband going home to his wife naked.
And she's outraged, and he explained that he just ran into somebody who took his clothes.
I had-- they-- they never took my clothes.
But I remember on two occasions, some very large, muscular, hefty Russian military women expressed some interest in me.
And I ran like a jackrabbit.
And I ran faster than they did.
So I retained my-- my-- my dignity.
But these are very lively times and very turbulent times.
And-- and after a few months, school reopened, and I went back and finished my high school education.
Then I was-- then I applied, ironically, first to the Medical University of Budapest.
I was accepted, and I attended medical school very briefly.
And-- and then when we were shown cadavers and-- and skeletons, I decided that this is not for me, and I switched over to the economics university.
And at that point came, probably, the turning point of my life.
I saw a sign saying that the B'nai B'rith Hillel Foundation is offering some scholarships to students who can speak English and who are interested in going to an American university.
And I applied, never dreaming that I would have a chance.
But I did.
Well, I-- I lived-- my own home, which was really an apartment, was totally destroyed by aerial bombing during the war.
It was a-- a six-story building near the elementary school where I lived, called Island Street, [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian.
And I remember the day-- we were in the bomb shelter.
This was-- this was before 1944 when Budapest was heavily bombed and-- and hit.
There was a direct hit on the apartment house.
We were in the basement.
As a matter of fact, we had to break through various basement doors to other apartments, and we came out several houses away.
So my home, as such, no longer existed.
Yes, I did.
Yes, I did.
You know, this sounds very strange for a 17-year-old boy, but I was always enormously self-reliant.
I was always a take-charge person.
I never, for a moment, asked what will happen to me, who will take care of me?
I had-- I felt I had a responsibility to take care of my aunt and uncle and other older people I knew.
And when I came to this country, although I never had a-- I never had any money, when I came to this country in August of '47, within about three months, I had seven different part-time jobs.
And I tell you, as best I can remember, what they were, and then I tell you what I did with my money, because it's sort of interesting.
I worked in a grocery store at night filling up the shelves.
I worked in a furniture store helping to carry furniture.
Then later on, I also did some bookkeeping for the furniture store.
I tutored Latin, math, English, French, and Italian.
I had a job in a borrowed tuxedo ushering at the Seattle Symphony.
And when Toscanini was at the symphony, I was one of the-- maybe the only person who spoke Italian, and I had the pleasure of having dinner with him because I spoke Italian.
I'll never forget it.
It was-- I-- he was in his 80s, I believe.
I was the sandwich man on Sorority Row.
I had a big tray on my neck with Washington state Delicious apples, pies, milk, and various sandwiches.
And it was a wonderful job.
I would go into the sororities filled with beautiful young women, and I screamed at the top of my voice, sandwich man, sandwich man.
And they would all come down, and I got more dates than I could handle.
And I made a fortune selling all this stuff.
When I got my first little money-- I have no idea what it was-- $20, $30.
I had a wonderful friend who is still my closest friend, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Jew from Alaska by the name of Barney Gutstein.
And he's my dearest and closest friend and has been since August of '47, almost 50 years now.
And he really taught me to be an American in every way.
Politically, how to drive a car, you name it.
When I got this first batch of fortune, he said, well, you have a lot of money now.
What do you want to do?
And I said, I want to go to the grocery store.
He said, fine.
And we drove to, I believe, a Safeway store, and I bought-- I can see this as clearly as I can see you-- I bought a pound of cocoa, a pound of chocolate, a pound of tea, and a pound of coffee.
And I paid for it, and we left.
He said, what-- where do you want to go now?
And I said, I want to go to another grocery store.
And he is a very patient man.
He still is very patient with me.
And he looked at me as if I would be somebody insane, but he took me to another grocery store a few blocks away.
I bought exactly the same thing.
We came out, and he said, where do we go now?
I said, I want to go to a grocery store.
And he said, now listen.
I'm a patient guy, but I've had enough.
Why don't you buy all the damn coffee and cocoa and everything you want to buy at one place.
And I said, I don't want people to think I am hoarding scarce goods.
And then he said, well, I'm not going to take you to a grocery store, but I'm going to buy you a cup of coffee.
And I'll explain to you about this country and buying scarce goods.
And he took me to a little coffee shop at the University District in Seattle.
And he explained to me that this is a free country.
Nobody cares whether I buy one pound of coffee or two pounds of coffee.
And nobody will spy on me and report me to the authorities that I'm hording coffee.
Of course, I bought all this to make packages with, to send it home to the people that I was supporting.
And this has now diminished to, literally, two people.
Everybody else died.
But in my own very modest way, I started out helping a little over a dozen people.
And, you know, I was helping them in terms of my resources.
Initially, I was sending $10, maybe.
And-- and occasionally, and as time went on and I got a regular job and so on, I was sending what to me were large sums-- $100, $200.
But I'm telling you all this because your-- your question was, was I worried about my future.
And I was never worried about my future.
